parts.answer: Knowledge 1, Application 2, Analysis 1 Quantitative skills assessed: QS5: Calculate cost, revenue, profit and break-even Knowledge 1 mark for identifying the formula for calculating margin of safety: • Actual sales level – break-even point (1) Application Up to 2 marks for applying the correct data: • 198 children/places - (1) (£952/£8.50) (1) OR • 198 children/places - (1) 112 children/places (1) Analysis 1 mark for calculating the margin of safety: • 86 children/places (1) NB: If no working is shown, award marks as follows: • If the answer given is 86 children/places award 4 marks • If the answer given is 86 award 3 marks (4) Question Analyse two economic variables that could lead to a reduction in the sales forecasts of an Adventure Babies’ franchisee, such as Jennifer. Mark Knowledge Up to 2 marks for defining economic variables, e.g. • Indicators that show (1) current trends in a market (1) OR For stating up to two economic variables, e.g. • Inflation (1) • Interest rates (1) Application Up to 2 marks for answers contextualised to Adventure Babies, e.g. • A franchisee needs to pay for the premises in which the classes are held (1) • The price for a place in one of Jennifer’s classes in 2023 was £8.50 (1) Analysis Up to 2 marks for reasons/causes/consequences for Adventure Babies, e.g. • A general rise in prices may increase this cost meaning the franchisee may need to raise the price to attend a class (1) • Higher interest rates may reduce the disposable income of parents paying off a mortgage/loans, meaning they can no longer afford this price (1) (6) Question Discuss the benefits for Jennifer of being an Adventure Babies’ franchisee. Indicative content Answers must be credited by using the level descriptors (below) in line with the general marking guidance. The indicative content below exemplifies some of the points that candidates may make but this does not imply that any of these must be included. Other relevant points must also be credited. Knowledge, Appl

Knowledge 1, Application 2, Analysis 1 Quantitative skills assessed: QS2: Calculate, use and understand percentages and percentage changes QS5: Calculate cost, revenue, profit and break-even Knowledge 1 mark for identifying the formula: • (Price x fee) x number of bookings (1) Application Up to 2 marks for selecting the correct data from Extract C: • $1.02 (1) x 175 (1) OR • $1 050 (1) x 17% (1) Analysis 1 mark for calculating the total fee: • = $178.50 (1) NB: If no working is shown, award marks as follows: • If the answer given is $178.50 award 4 marks • If the answer given is 178.50 award 3 marks (4) Question Analyse two reasons why productivity may have improved at the Belmont Estate. Mark Knowledge Up to 2 marks for defining productivity, e.g. • The rate of output (1) per unit of input (1) OR For stating up to two reasons, e.g. • Motivated employees (1) • Flexibility of the workforce (1) Application Up to 2 marks for answers contextualised to Belmont Estate, e.g. • Belmont Estate is a happy and positive place to work (1) • The batch production process is well-organised (1) Analysis Up to 2 marks for reasons/causes/consequences for Belmont Estate, e.g. • Motivated employees can lead to a faster rate of production (1) • Efficient production means less time is needed between stages of production (1) (6) Question Discuss how increasing the price of Belmont Estate’s products may increase its profit. Indicative content Answers must be credited by using the level descriptors (below) in line with the general marking guidance. The indicative content below exemplifies some of the points that candidates may make but this does not imply that any of these must be included. Other relevant points must also be credited. Knowledge, Application, Analysis, Evaluation – indicative content • Profit is the money made by a business after total costs are deducted from total revenue • If Belmont Estate raises its price from $7.50, it will receive more revenue per bar of chocolate sold
